1: to Arnion hestōs epi to oros Siōn: the Lamb standing (hestōs — perfect participle, standing in the resurrection state, same as 5:6) on Mount Zion. The contrast with 13:1 (beast from the sea) is deliberate: where the beast rises from the chaos of the sea, the Lamb stands on Zion — the OT eschatological locus of divine deliverance (Ps 2:6; 48:2; Isa 4:5; Joel 2:32; Obad 17, 21; Mic 4:7). Zion was the place where God enthroned his king (Ps 2:6); now the Lamb stands there as the enthroned conqueror. The 144,000 with the Lamb bear to onoma autou kai to onoma tou patros autou (his name and his Father's name) on their foreheads — the seal of divine ownership contrasting with the beast's mark.
2: phōnēn ek tou ouranou hōs phōnēn hydatōn pollōn kai hōs phōnēn brontēs megalēs: "like the sound of many waters and of loud thunder." This triple simile for the heavenly song echoes Ezek 1:24 (the sound of many waters, like the voice of the Almighty, when they moved). The sound is described before the singers are identified, emphasizing the overwhelming auditory reality of the heavenly worship. Hōs kitharōdōn kitharizontōn en tais kitharais autōn: like harpers harping — the harp (kithara) is the instrument of praise (Ps 33:2; 150:3).
3: ōdēn kainēn: a new song. The "new song" (ōdē kainē) in the Psalter (Ps 33:3; 40:3; 96:1; 98:1; 144:9; 149:1; Isa 42:10) always celebrates a new act of divine salvation — a song specific to this redemption, not a recycled doxology. Oudeis edynato mathein tēn ōdēn: no one could learn (manthanō, to learn by study and practice) the song except the 144,000 — because the song expresses their specific experience. Redemption gives knowledge that cannot be acquired otherwise; only the redeemed-through-tribulation can sing the redeemed-through-tribulation song.
4:
houtoi eisin hoi meta gynaikōn ouk emolynthēsan parthenoi gar eisin: these are those who have not defiled themselves with women, for they are virgins. The porneia/purity language in Revelation is consistently used for spiritual apostasy and idolatry (the great prostitute of ch. 17; Jezebel in 2:20-22; Babylon making all nations drink the wine of her sexual immorality). The 144,000's virgin purity (parthenoi) = they have not committed spiritual adultery with the beast-cult. In prophetic literature, idolatry is consistently framed as harlotry (Hos 1-3; Ezek 16; 23; Jer 2-3). Literal celibacy is possible but less consistent with Revelation's symbolic register.

aparchē: firstfruits — the 144,000 are the firstfruits (aparchē) of God's great harvest. The harvest metaphor unites the 144,000 (firstfruits, v. 4) with the great grain and grape harvest of vv. 14-20: they are the beginning of the full redeemed humanity.
5: en tō stomati autōn ouch heurethē pseudos: in their mouth no lie was found. Pseudos (falsehood, lie) is the defining characteristic of the beast's false prophet (13:14: plana) and of those excluded from the new Jerusalem (21:27; 22:15). The 144,000's freedom from falsehood marks them as authentic witnesses — their testimony (martyria) is truthful because they have not compromised with the beast's deceptions. Amōmoi: blameless, without blemish — the same word used for the unblemished sacrificial animal (Num 19:2 LXX; Heb 9:14; 1 Pet 1:19). The 144,000 are themselves the spotless offering — they are both worshippers and the sacrifice.
6: euangelion aiōnion: the eternal gospel (euangelion aiōnion) — the only occurrence of this phrase in the NT. Aiōnios (eternal, age-spanning) qualifies the gospel as transcending any particular historical moment: the good news of God's reign is not time-conditioned. The angel proclaims it to epi panta ethnē kai phylas kai glōssas kai laous (every nation, tribe, tongue, and people) — the fourfold universal formula from Dan 3:7 LXX, marking the complete universality of the proclamation. The scope of the gospel matches the scope of the beast's dominion (13:7) — every people group hears.
7: phobēthēte ton theon kai dote autō doxan: Fear God and give him glory — two imperatives establishing the fundamental posture of true worship. Phobeomai (reverential awe, not mere terror) and didōmi doxan (give glory, attribute honour) are both explicitly commanded in contrast to the earth-dwellers' reflexive glorification of the beast (13:4). Hoti ēlthen hē hōra tēs kriseōs autou: the hour of his judgment has come — ēlthen aorist: it has arrived. The eschatological hour is here, not impending. The gospel call goes out precisely because judgment is imminent: the euangelion and the krisis are simultaneous.
8: Epesen, epesen Babulōn hē megalē: "Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great" — the doubled epesen (fell, fell) reproduces exactly the LXX form of Isa 21:9 (fallen is Babylon; and all the carved images of her gods he has shattered to the ground) and Jer 51:8 (suddenly Babylon has fallen and been broken). The prophetic perfect: the fall is announced as already accomplished from the heavenly perspective before it is historically described in chs. 17-18. Hē ek tou oinou tou thymou tēs porneias autēs pepotiken panta ta ethnē: the wine of the passion of her sexual immorality — Babylon's seduction is economic and religious intoxication that makes resistance seem impossible.
9: ei tis proskunei to thērion kai tēn eikona autou: if anyone worships the beast and its image — the condition for receiving God's wrath. The three-part object (beast, image, mark) = the full apparatus of the beast-cult: the political power (beast), the religious apparatus (image), and the economic integration (mark). Worship of any one implies the full system. The third angel's proclamation (vv. 9-11) is the most severe warning in Revelation, matching the severity of the beast's claim.
10: pinei ek tou oinou tou thymou tou theou tou kekerasmenou akratou: he will drink the wine of God's wrath poured full strength (akraton = unmixed, undiluted — wine was normally diluted with water; undiluted wine = maximum potency). The cup of divine wrath from Ps 75:8; Isa 51:17; Jer 25:15-17 is here poured akraton — no dilution, no mitigation. En pyri kai theiō: with fire and sulfur — the Sodom judgment formula (Gen 19:24; Ezek 38:22). The torment is not incidental but the just response to beast-worship.
11: ho kapnos tou basanismou autōn eis aiōnas aiōnōn anabainei: the smoke of their torment ascends forever and ever. This echoes Isa 34:10 (Edom's judgment: its smoke shall go up forever; from generation to generation it shall lie waste) applied now to those who worship the beast at the final judgment. Ouk echousin anapausin hēmeras kai nyktos: they have no rest (anapausis) day or night — the permanent inversion of the Sabbath rest promised to the faithful (14:13). The eternal persistence of judgment is stated here with the strongest language in the NT.
12: hōde hē hypomonē tōn hagiōn estin: here (hōde) is the patient endurance of the saints. Hypomonē (steadfast endurance, active perseverance under pressure) is the virtue that connects 14:9-11 to 14:12: the eternal severity of judgment on beast-worshippers is the theological ground for the saints' endurance. Knowing what beast-worship costs (eternally) makes the temporal cost of refusal bearable. Hoi tērountes tas entolas tou theou kai tēn pistin Iēsou: those who keep God's commandments and the faith of Jesus — both commitments define the saint who endures.
13: Makarioi hoi nekroi hoi en Kyriō apothnēskontes ap' arti: "Blessed are the dead who die in the Lord from now on." The third beatitude in Revelation (1:3; 14:13; 16:15; 19:9; 20:6; 22:7, 14). Ap' arti (from now on) — specifically the martyrs of the eschatological tribulation. Nai, legei to Pneuma: Yes, says the Spirit — the Spirit authenticates the beatitude with divine affirmation. Hina anapausontai ek tōn kopōn autōn: they rest (anapauō) from their labors — the same rest denied to beast-worshippers (v. 11). The two destinies: rest for the faithful dead, no rest for beast-worshippers.
14: homoion hyion anthrōpou: one like a son of man — the Danielic figure of Dan 7:13 (one like a son of man came with the clouds of heaven and came to the Ancient of Days) now enthroned on a white cloud. The golden crown (stephanos chrysous) signals royal authority; the sharp sickle (drepanon oxy) is the harvest tool. The figure is Christ himself — combining Dan 7's Son of Man with Joel 3:13 (put in the sickle, for the harvest is ripe). The harvest image from the Parable of the Weeds (Matt 13:39-43) is here expanded to cosmic scale.
15: ēlthen hē hōra therisai: the hour to reap has come — ēlthen aorist: arrived. Exēranthē ho therismos tēs gēs: the harvest of the earth is ripe/dried out. Xērainō (to dry, wither) applied to grain means it has reached full maturity and is ready to harvest — not rotting but fully ripe. The harvest language echoes Isa 17:5 (the gleanings of the grain), Joel 3:13 (the harvest is ripe, go in and tread, for the winepress is full), and Matt 13:39-43. This grain harvest (vv. 15-16) represents the gathering of the righteous.
16: ebalen ho kathēmenos epi tēs nephelēs to drepanon autou epi tēn gēn: the one seated on the cloud swung (ebalon) his sickle over the earth, and the earth was reaped (etheristhē). The brevity of the description signals the sovereignty and efficiency of divine judgment: a single act, worldwide scope, instantaneous completion. Etheristhē — the harvest passive: the earth is harvested as the object, not the agent. The great reaping of history comes to completion in one decisive act.
17: allos angelos exēlthen ek tou naou tou en tō ouranō: another angel came out of the temple (naos) in heaven. The grape harvest angel (vv. 17-20) is distinct from the one-like-a-son-of-man (vv. 14-16), and both come from the heavenly temple — the source of the eschatological harvest is divine, authorized from the heavenly sanctuary. The naos (inner sanctuary) houses the divine presence; these angels emerge from the place of God's holiness to execute the harvest of judgment.
18: allos angelos ek tou thusiastēriou: another angel from the altar (thusiastērion). This is the altar that holds the souls of the martyred (6:9-10) — those who cried out for justice. The angel who has authority over fire (exousian epi tou pyros) calls for the grape harvest. The fire-angel from the altar of incense (8:3-5) earlier hurled fire to earth as divine judgment; now the altar-angel summons the grape harvest of wrath. The martyrs' cry for justice (6:10: "How long?") is being answered.
19: tēn lēnon tou thymou tou theou ton megan: the great winepress of the fury (thymos) of God — the OT winepress of judgment from Isa 63:3 (I have trodden the winepress alone; from the peoples no one was with me) and Joel 3:13 (tread, for the winepress is full). The one who treads the winepress in Isa 63 is YHWH himself, the divine warrior; in Rev 19:15 Christ treads the winepress. The judgment is not impersonal force but the direct action of the holy God against evil.
20: exō tēs poleōs: outside the city. In Isa 63:1-6, the divine warrior comes from Edom, outside Jerusalem. More significantly, Christ was crucified outside the city walls (John 19:20; Heb 13:12-13) — the blood of Calvary shed outside Jerusalem prefigures the blood of divine judgment poured out outside. Haima ek tēs lēnou achri tōn chalinōn tōn hippōn apo stadiōn chiliadōn hexakosiōn deka tessarōn: blood up to the horses' bridles for 1,600 stadia. 1,600 = 4 × 4 × 100 — the number four (four corners of the earth) squared and magnified a hundredfold: universal, complete, maximal judgment.
